reasoning: The base rate of the WHO declaring a new pandemic is around 2-5% per year, based on the occurrence of pandemics over the last century (e.g., 2009 H1N1, 2020 COVID-19). Currently, while pathogens like H5N1 avian influenza are closely monitored due to their spread in animal populations and isolated human cases, there is no sustained human-to-human transmission. The ongoing Mpox outbreak was declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), but the WHO historically reserves the formal designation of 'pandemic' for rapidly spreading, novel diseases with global H2H transmission (typically respiratory). Without a current, rapidly escalating global outbreak of a novel respiratory or equivalent pathogen, the probability of a new pandemic declaration specifically in the calendar year 2025 remains very low, aligns with the historical base rate, and is estimated at 3%.
